question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send repeated rapid POST requests to /network/status to exploit the race condition between n.RetrieveGenesis(ctx) and n.RetrieveLatest(ctx) calls, causing the returned GenesisBlockIdentifier and CurrentBlockIdentifier to reflect inconsistent chain states (genesis from block N, current from block N-1), leading to a client-visible reorg of the canonical chain tip?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where currentBlock.GetRosettaBlockIdentifier() returns a block index lower than genesisBlock.GetRosettaBlockIdentifier() index by flooding /network/status during a genesis re-initialization, causing downstream Rosetta clients to treat the current chain as shorter than genesis and discard valid transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of cross-validation between genesisBlock.GetRosettaBlockIdentifier() and currentBlock.GetRosettaBlockIdentifier() in NetworkStatus() to observe a response where GenesisBlockIdentifier.Index > CurrentBlockIdentifier.Index, causing Rosetta clients to believe the entire transaction history is invalid and triggering a full chain resync?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send a crafted /network/status request during a database failover window, causing n.RetrieveGenesis(ctx) to succeed with a cached stale genesis block while n.RetrieveLatest(ctx) returns a fresh block from the new primary, producing a GenesisBlockIdentifier that does not belong to the same chain as CurrentBlockIdentifier and reorganizing the apparent chain history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that n.RetrieveGenesis(ctx) uses a cached genesisBlock (from blockRepository.initGenesisRecordFile) while n.RetrieveLatest(ctx) always queries live data, to observe a NetworkStatusResponse where the GenesisBlockIdentifier hash does not match any block in the live chain, causing Rosetta clients to reorganize their view of transaction history from a phantom genesis?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/options and observe that HistoricalBalanceLookup is hardcoded to true regardless of whether the underlying persistence layer actually supports historical queries, causing Rosetta clients to issue historical balance queries that return incorrect data and misattribute transaction history to wrong blocks?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the non-deterministic iteration order of types.GetTransactionResults() map in NetworkOptions() to observe different orderings of operationStatuses across requests, causing Rosetta clients that rely on status ordering to misclassify transaction results and reorganize which transactions appear as successful vs failed in history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/options and observe that persistence.IsTransactionResultSuccessful() marks result code 104 (FEE_SCHEDULE_FILE_PART_UPLOADED) as Successful=true, causing Rosetta clients to include partial file upload operations as successful transfers in transaction history, reorganizing the apparent set of completed transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that operationStatuses in NetworkOptions() includes result code -1 (generalErrorStatus) mapped to a non-standard status string, causing Rosetta clients to misinterpret unknown transaction results as a specific known status and incorrectly reorganize failed transactions into the successful history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/options repeatedly and observe that the operationTypes slice includes types.OperationTypeFee appended after GetStringValuesFromInt32StringMap(), potentially creating a duplicate OperationTypeFee entry if the underlying map already contains it, causing Rosetta clients to double-count fee operations in transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any NetworkRequest validation in NetworkStatus() (the request parameter is ignored with _) to send a request with a mismatched network_identifier, causing the service to return status for a different network than requested and presenting a reorganized transaction history from the wrong chain?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkList()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/list and observe that only a single NetworkIdentifier is returned without any chain ID or genesis hash binding, allowing a client to be redirected to a different network's /network/status endpoint and receive a completely different transaction history as if it were the canonical chain?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that currentBlock.GetTimestampMillis() is returned as CurrentBlockTimestamp without validation against the GenesisBlockIdentifier timestamp, causing Rosetta clients to accept a current block timestamp earlier than genesis and reorganize the apparent chronological order of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a context cancellation (via slow network or abrupt disconnect) mid-way through NetworkStatus() after RetrieveGenesis() succeeds but before RetrieveLatest() completes, causing a partial response or error that leaves the client's cached chain state with only a genesis block and no current tip, effectively reorganizing the client's view to genesis?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential (non-atomic) calls to n.RetrieveGenesis(ctx) and n.RetrieveLatest(ctx) in NetworkStatus() during a block pruning or reindex event, causing the genesis block to be re-anchored to a later timestamp while the current block still reflects the old chain, presenting a reorganized history window to Rosetta clients?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent /network/status requests to exploit a TOCTOU window between the genesis block cache initialization (initGenesisRecordFile) and the latest block query (selectLatestWithIndex), causing different concurrent responses to return different genesis/current block pairs that imply different chain reorganization points?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that peers.ToRosetta() in NetworkStatus() returns node endpoints without any integrity verification, causing a Rosetta client that uses the peer list to sync transaction history to connect to a malicious peer and receive a reorganized transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a prev_hash chain validation between genesisBlock and currentBlock in NetworkStatus(), causing the response to present a GenesisBlockIdentifier and CurrentBlockIdentifier that belong to different forks, reorganizing the apparent canonical chain for any client that trusts this endpoint?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/options and observe that errors.Errors is returned as a static slice without any request-specific filtering, causing Rosetta clients to accept error codes that should be retired or deprecated and misclassify historical transaction failures, reorganizing which transactions appear as errors vs successes?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NewNetworkAPIService()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that operationTypes is built once at construction time via tools.GetStringValuesFromInt32StringMap(types.GetTransactionTypes()) and never refreshed, causing the /network/options endpoint to advertise stale operation types after a protocol upgrade, leading Rosetta clients to misclassify new transaction types in history as unknown and reorganize their categorization?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the int64 block index returned by currentBlock.GetRosettaBlockIdentifier() overflowing when cast to int32 in downstream consumers (as evidenced by TestRetrieveGenesisIndexOverflowInt4), causing the block index to wrap to a negative or small value and making Rosetta clients believe the chain has reorganized to a much earlier block?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send a /network/status request when the mirror node is mid-sync, causing n.RetrieveLatest(ctx) to return a block whose index is less than the previously advertised current block, presenting a chain reorganization (apparent rollback) to any Rosetta client that compares successive /network/status responses?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that addressBookEntryRepo.Entries(ctx) queries the latest address book (max(start_consensus_timestamp)) without pinning to the current block's consensus timestamp, causing the peers list to reflect a future or past network topology that does not correspond to the CurrentBlockIdentifier, misleading clients about which peers hold the canonical transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/options and observe that the Allow.OperationStatuses slice contains duplicate status names (e.g., if two result codes map to the same string in GetTransactionResults()), causing Rosetta clients to fail validation and fall back to treating all transactions as having unknown status, reorganizing the entire transaction history classification?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of monotonicity enforcement on CurrentBlockTimestamp in NetworkStatus(), causing a response where GetTimestampMillis() returns a timestamp earlier than a previously returned value (due to clock skew or block reprocessing), making Rosetta clients believe a time-based chain reorganization has occurred?
